choices.text: If $VAR is unset or empty, the command becomes `rm -rf /` — which (run as root) wipes the entire filesystem. Many famous outages and personal disasters trace to this single bug pattern. Defensive habits: (1) always set `set -u` (treat unset variables as errors) in scripts, (2) quote variables: `rm -rf "$VAR/"` doesn't help here directly but prevents word-splitting bugs, (3) guard explicitly: `[ -n "$VAR" ] || exit 1` before destructive operations, (4) consider `: ${VAR:?"VAR must be set"}` to require it, (5) prefer `find $VAR -delete` for explicit traversal that fails on bad input. Treat any rm -rf with variables as a potential disaster site.

questions.explanation: Empty $VAR makes it `rm -rf /`. Always set -u, guard with checks, prefer find -delete.

questions.prompt: You want a 'current' symlink pointing to /opt/app-v3 that you can later flip to /opt/app-v4 atomically. Command?

choices.text: `ln -s /opt/app-v3 /opt/app/current` — creates a symbolic link that consumers reference. To upgrade: `ln -snf /opt/app-v4 /opt/app/current` atomically replaces the symlink (the `-f` forces overwrite; `-n` prevents the new target being created inside the directory the old symlink points to). Process: deploy v4 alongside v3, flip the symlink, restart services pointing at /opt/app/current. Rollback: flip back to v3. This is the deploy pattern used by Capistrano and similar tools. Symlinks make 'which version is live' a single atomic operation rather than a copy + replace dance that risks half-updated state during the swap.

questions.explanation: ln -s for symlink; flip with `ln -snf new_target current` atomically on deploy.
